on_label: Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) in patients aged 6 years and older
off_label: Fatigue in cancer or palliative care
Cognitive enhancement in traumatic brain injury

medications.mechanism_of_action: Blocks the reuptake of norepinephrine and dopamine into the presynaptic neuron and increases the release of these monoamines into the extraneuronal space. The d-threo enantiomer is the more pharmacologically active isomer.
medications.time_to_max_concentration: 6-10 hours (Concerta/OROS); 1-2 hours initial peak then 4-7 hours second peak (Ritalin LA/Metadate CD)
medications.half_life: 3.5 hours (Concerta); 6.8 hours (apparent, extended-release capsule formulations due to absorption-rate-limited elimination)
medications.duration_of_action: 8-12 hours (Concerta/OROS: ~12 hours; Ritalin LA/Metadate CD: ~8 hours)
medications.active_metabolites: None clinically significant. Ritalinic acid (alpha-phenyl-piperidine acetic acid/PPA) is the primary metabolite but has little or no pharmacologic activity.
medications.half_life_of_active_metabolites: 3-4 hours (ritalinic acid, but not pharmacologically active)

interactions_with_other_drugs_on_this_li.interaction: Additive CNS stimulant effects; concurrent use of two stimulants increases risk of cardiovascular effects (hypertension, tachycardia), insomnia, and CNS overstimulation. Generally not prescribed together at full doses.
Bupropion lowers the seizure threshold; combined use with stimulants may further increase seizure risk. Additive dopaminergic and noradrenergic effects.
Methylphenidate may inhibit the metabolism of SSRIs. Rare risk of serotonin syndrome with combined serotonergic activity. Monitor for increased sertraline side effects.

Proton pump inhibitors may alter absorption characteristics of some extended-release methylphenidate formulations by changing gastric pH, though clinical significance is generally low for OROS formulations.
Opposing cardiovascular effects; propranolol may mitigate stimulant-induced tachycardia/hypertension. This combination is sometimes used therapeutically but requires monitoring.
Additive CNS-stimulant effects. Excessive caffeine may contribute to nervousness, irritability, insomnia, or tremor during concurrent use.
Methylphenidate can potentiate the pressor actions of vasopressors like ephedrine. Monitor cardiac function.
PPIs raise gastric pH, which may alter absorption characteristics of some extended-release methylphenidate formulations. Clinical significance is generally low for OROS-type formulations.

medications.administration: May be taken with or without food. Swallow whole; do not crush, chew, or break extended-release tablets/capsules. Concerta tablets should be taken with liquid. Capsule formulations (Ritalin LA, Metadate CD, Aptensio XR) may be opened and sprinkled on applesauce; do not crush or chew beads.

medications.mechanism_of_action: Non-catecholamine sympathomimetic amine. Blocks reuptake of norepinephrine and dopamine into presynaptic neurons and increases release of these monoamines into the extraneuronal space. Also has minor effects on serotonin. Contains d-amphetamine and l-amphetamine salts in a 3:1 ratio.
medications.duration_of_action: 8-12 hours
medications.active_metabolites: 4-hydroxyamphetamine, alpha-hydroxyamphetamine, norephedrine. These metabolites have some pharmacologic activity but contribute minimally to overall clinical effect at therapeutic doses.
medications.half_life_of_active_metabolites: Not well characterized independently; urinary elimination is pH-dependent

interactions_with_other_drugs_on_this_li.interaction: Additive CNS stimulant effects; increased risk of cardiovascular adverse events and CNS overstimulation.

Proton pump inhibitors reduce gastric acidity, which may shorten Tmax of amphetamine ER capsules (median Tmax shortened from 5 hours to 2.75 hours). May lead to earlier and higher peak concentrations.
Opposing cardiovascular effects. Propranolol may attenuate amphetamine-induced tachycardia and hypertension.
Trazodone may cause false-positive amphetamine screens. Additive effects on monoamine systems.
H2 blockers may raise gastric pH, potentially altering amphetamine absorption (though less effect than PPIs).
Additive CNS-stimulant effects. Patients should limit caffeine intake to avoid nervousness, irritability, insomnia, or tremor.
Cimetidine may raise gastrointestinal pH, potentially increasing amphetamine absorption and exacerbating stimulant effects.
Additive sympathomimetic effects. Increased cardiovascular risk (hypertension, tachycardia).

Amphetamines may pharmacodynamically counteract the sedative properties of chlorpheniramine. Alternatively, chlorpheniramine may blunt the stimulant effects of amphetamines used for narcolepsy or ADHD.
PPIs reduce gastric acidity, which may shorten the Tmax of extended-release amphetamine formulations (median Tmax may be shortened from ~5 hours to ~2.75 hours), leading to an earlier peak effect.

medications.mechanism_of_action: Partial agonist at serotonin 5-HT1A receptors (both presynaptic autoreceptors and postsynaptic receptors). Moderate affinity for brain D2 dopamine receptors. Does not bind to benzodiazepine receptors or affect GABA binding. Metabolized by CYP3A4.
medications.duration_of_action: 2-3 hours per dose; full anxiolytic effect requires 2-4 weeks of continuous dosing
medications.active_metabolites: 1-pyrimidinylpiperazine (1-PP) — potent alpha-2 adrenergic antagonist which may contribute to pharmacological effects. 6-hydroxybuspirone is also an active metabolite (5-HT1A partial agonist) with plasma levels 40-fold higher than parent.
medications.half_life_of_active_metabolites: 1-PP: approximately 6 hours; 6-hydroxybuspirone: data limited but longer than parent compound

interactions_with_other_drugs_on_this_li.interaction: Additive serotonergic effects; theoretical risk of serotonin syndrome, though this combination is commonly used clinically for augmentation. Monitor for serotonin syndrome symptoms.
One report of concomitant use causing 3- to 6-fold elevations in SGPT (ALT). Additive serotonergic effects and theoretical serotonin syndrome risk. Monitor liver function.
In vitro studies show buspirone does not displace propranolol from plasma proteins. No significant pharmacokinetic interaction expected at therapeutic levels, but both may have additive CNS effects.
No significant known interaction.
No well-documented pharmacokinetic interaction. Both affect monoamine systems through different mechanisms.
Additive serotonergic effects; monitor for serotonin syndrome.
Additive CNS depressant effects possible. Monitor for increased sedation.
Additive CNS depression possible. Buspirone does not enhance benzodiazepine activity at GABA-A receptors, but combined anxiolytic and sedative effects may occur.
Additive CNS depression possible. Buspirone does not enhance benzodiazepine effects at GABA-A receptors, but additive sedative effects may occur.

Additive CNS depression possible. Conversion from benzodiazepine to buspirone therapy may require treatment overlap, but the combination can produce additive sedative effects. Buspirone does not enhance benzodiazepine effects at GABA-A receptors.

medications.administration: Take consistently with or without food. Food increases bioavailability; taking with food is generally preferred. Take at the same time(s) each day for consistent blood levels.

medications.mechanism_of_action: Norepinephrine-dopamine reuptake inhibitor (NDRI). Weakly inhibits reuptake of norepinephrine and dopamine. Does not inhibit monoamine oxidase. Mechanism of antidepressant activity is not fully understood. Also a nicotinic acetylcholine receptor antagonist. Is a potent CYP2D6 inhibitor.
medications.duration_of_action: 1-2 days (XL: 24 hours; SR: ~12 hours)
medications.active_metabolites: Hydroxybupropion (primary, via CYP2B6), threohydrobupropion, erythrohydrobupropion. Hydroxybupropion has approximately half the potency of bupropion as an antidepressant.
medications.half_life_of_active_metabolites: Hydroxybupropion: ~20 hours; Threohydrobupropion: ~37 hours; Erythrohydrobupropion: ~33 hours

interactions_with_other_drugs_on_this_li.interaction: Additive dopaminergic and noradrenergic effects. Bupropion lowers seizure threshold; use caution.
Additive dopaminergic effects. Increased seizure risk. Monitor cardiovascular parameters.
Bupropion is a potent CYP2D6 inhibitor and may increase sertraline levels. Monitor for increased SSRI side effects.
Bupropion (CYP2D6 inhibitor) may increase propranolol levels, as propranolol is partially metabolized by CYP2D6. Monitor for bradycardia and hypotension.
Bupropion is a potent CYP2D6 inhibitor and significantly increases dextromethorphan levels. Risk of DXM toxicity and serotonin syndrome. Avoid or use with extreme caution.
Bupropion may inhibit CYP2D6-mediated metabolism of trazodone (though trazodone is primarily CYP3A4-metabolized). Additive seizure risk.
Bupropion may inhibit CYP2D6-mediated metabolism of ondansetron. Clinical significance is uncertain.
Bupropion may lower seizure threshold; hydroxyzine at high doses may also lower seizure threshold. Additive risk.
Excessive caffeine may increase seizure risk with bupropion. Monitor for irritability, tremor, increased blood pressure, and insomnia.
Additive noradrenergic effects. Potential for increased blood pressure and seizure risk.
Meclizine may affect seizure threshold when combined with bupropion.
Diphenhydramine component of dimenhydrinate inhibits CYP2D6 and may increase bupropion levels.

Bupropion is a potent CYP2D6 inhibitor; chlorpheniramine is a CYP2D6 substrate. Concurrent use may increase chlorpheniramine plasma levels and toxicity. Also, bupropion lowers the seizure threshold; monitor for increased side effects.
Bupropion may inhibit CYP2D6, a minor metabolic pathway for zolpidem. Monitor for increased zolpidem CNS effects.
Bupropion lowers the seizure threshold; benzodiazepines are anticonvulsants that may partially mitigate this risk. Monitor for CNS effects.

Bupropion is contraindicated in patients undergoing abrupt benzodiazepine withdrawal due to increased seizure risk. Benzodiazepines have anticonvulsant properties that may partially offset bupropion's seizure threshold-lowering effect. Monitor for CNS effects.

medications.mechanism_of_action: Bile acid sequestrant. Binds bile acids in the intestine to form an insoluble complex that is excreted in feces. This removes bile acids from the enterohepatic circulation, promoting hepatic conversion of cholesterol to bile acids, ultimately lowering LDL cholesterol levels. Not absorbed systemically.
medications.time_to_max_concentration: Not applicable — colestipol is not absorbed systemically
medications.duration_of_action: 12-24 hours (not absorbed systemically; acts locally in the GI tract by binding bile acids)
medications.active_metabolites: None — not absorbed or metabolized systemically

May reduce sertraline absorption. Separate administration by at least 1 hour before or 4 hours after colestipol.
Colestipol may reduce propranolol absorption. Separate administration times.
Classic interaction — significantly reduces thyroid hormone absorption.
May reduce absorption. Separate dosing.

Colestipol (bile acid sequestrant) may bind pantoprazole in the GI tract and reduce its absorption. Separate doses by at least 1-2 hours.

medications.administration: Take with meals and a full glass of water or other liquid. Mix granules with at least 3 oz of liquid or a pulpy fruit. Take other medications at least 1 hour before or 4 hours after colestipol to avoid reduced absorption.

on_label: Male pattern hair loss/androgenetic alopecia (1mg dose)

medications.mechanism_of_action: Competitive and specific inhibitor of Type II 5-alpha reductase, an intracellular enzyme that converts testosterone to dihydrotestosterone (DHT). Reduces serum and tissue DHT concentrations.
medications.half_life: 6 hours (range 3-16 hours); in elderly (>70 years): approximately 8 hours
medications.duration_of_action: At least 24 hours (5-alpha reductase inhibition persists due to irreversible enzyme binding; DHT levels return to baseline within approximately 14 days of discontinuation)
medications.active_metabolites: Two metabolites have been identified (monohydroxylated and omega-carboxylic acid metabolites) with approximately 20% of finasteride's activity for 5-alpha reductase inhibition.

interactions_with_other_drugs_on_this_li.interaction: Colestipol may reduce finasteride absorption. Separate dosing times.

medications.duration_of_action: IR: 6-12 hours; LA/ER: up to 24 hours
medications.active_metabolites: 4-hydroxypropranolol — has beta-blocking activity comparable to propranolol. Formed via CYP2D6.

medications.mechanism_of_action: Proton pump inhibitor (PPI). Suppresses gastric acid secretion by specific inhibition of the H+/K+ ATPase enzyme system (proton pump) at the secretory surface of the gastric parietal cell. Esomeprazole is the S-isomer of omeprazole. Blocks the final step of acid production. Metabolized by CYP2C19 and CYP3A4.
medications.half_life: 1-1.5 hours (but duration of antisecretory effect lasts up to 24 hours due to irreversible binding to proton pump)
medications.duration_of_action: Up to 24 hours (antisecretory effect persists due to irreversible binding to the proton pump, despite a short plasma half-life)
medications.active_metabolites: None — metabolites (hydroxy and desmethyl metabolites) have no significant antisecretory activity

interactions_with_other_drugs_on_this_li.interaction: PPIs reduce gastric acidity, which may shorten Tmax of extended-release amphetamine capsules (median Tmax shortened from ~5 hours to ~2.75 hours), leading to earlier peak effect.
Dual acid suppression — generally redundant and not recommended. PPIs are superior to H2 blockers for sustained acid suppression.
Colestipol may bind esomeprazole and reduce absorption. Separate dosing.
Dual acid suppression is generally redundant. If both used, the H2 blocker at bedtime may help with nocturnal acid breakthrough.
Dual PPI therapy — redundant and not recommended. Both drugs irreversibly inhibit the gastric proton pump; concurrent use provides no additional benefit and increases adverse effect risk.
PPI provides gastroprotection during aspirin therapy. Beneficial combination to reduce risk of aspirin-induced GI bleeding, especially in patients on long-term aspirin for cardiovascular prophylaxis.

Esomeprazole inhibits CYP2C19 and weakly inhibits CYP3A4. Case reports describe interactions between PPIs and CYP3A4-metabolized benzodiazepines. Clinical significance is generally low but monitor for increased triazolam effects.

medications.active_metabolites: None significant — >80% excreted as unchanged drug in urine

medications.mechanism_of_action: Non-benzodiazepine hypnotic (imidazopyridine class). Preferentially binds the BZ1 (omega-1) subunit of the GABA-A receptor complex, enhancing GABAergic inhibitory effects and increasing chloride conductance, leading to sedation and sleep induction. Metabolized primarily by CYP3A4, and to a lesser extent by CYP2C9, CYP1A2, and CYP2D6.

Dual acid suppression is redundant. Cimetidine also inhibits CYP2C19 and CYP3A4, which are metabolic pathways for pantoprazole, potentially increasing pantoprazole exposure.
Colestipol (bile acid sequestrant) may bind pantoprazole in the GI tract and reduce its absorption. Separate doses by at least 1-2 hours.

PPIs raise gastric pH, which may alter the absorption characteristics of some extended-release methylphenidate formulations (e.g., OROS-type). Clinical significance is generally low for most ER formulations.
PPIs reduce gastric acidity, which may shorten the Tmax of extended-release amphetamine formulations (median Tmax may be shortened from ~5 hours to ~2.75 hours), leading to an earlier peak effect.

Pantoprazole is often co-prescribed with NSAIDs such as celecoxib to reduce the risk of NSAID-induced gastropathy and peptic ulcers. This is a pharmacologically beneficial combination. No adverse pharmacokinetic interaction.
Pantoprazole is often co-prescribed with NSAIDs to reduce risk of NSAID-induced gastrointestinal injury. Beneficial combination for GI protection.

medications.administration: Take 30-60 minutes before a meal, preferably before breakfast, for optimal efficacy. Swallow tablets whole; do not crush, split, or chew. Delayed-release granules for oral suspension may be mixed with apple juice or applesauce. May be taken with or without food, but pre-meal dosing is preferred for maximum efficacy.

medications.maximum_daily_dosage: 30 mg/night; 7.5-15 mg recommended for elderly or debilitated patients
medications.time_required_between_doses: Once nightly at bedtime; do not redose during the same night
medications.mechanism_of_action: Benzodiazepine hypnotic. Classified as a positive allosteric modulator of GABA-A receptors (GABA-PAM), with particular affinity for α1 subunits. Binds to benzodiazepine receptors on GABA-A chloride channel complexes in the CNS (limbic, thalamic, and hypothalamic regions), enhancing GABAergic inhibition by increasing chloride ion influx frequency. This leads to CNS depression, producing sedation, anxiolysis, skeletal muscle relaxation, and anticonvulsant effects. Metabolized primarily via direct glucuronide conjugation (Phase II); undergoes minimal CYP450 metabolism.
medications.half_life: 3.5-18.4 hours (terminal half-life, average ~8.8 hours); may be prolonged in elderly patients
medications.duration_of_action: 6-8 hours (hypnotic effect; suitable for sleep maintenance throughout the night)
medications.active_metabolites: Oxazepam (minor metabolite via parallel oxidation pathway) — pharmacologically active but present in small amounts. Temazepam glucuronide is the primary (inactive) metabolite.
medications.half_life_of_active_metabolites: Oxazepam: 5-15 hours (minor contribution to overall effect)

interactions_with_other_drugs_on_this_li.interaction: Duplicate benzodiazepine therapy. Concurrent use of two benzodiazepines significantly increases risk of profound CNS depression, respiratory depression, coma, and death. This combination should be avoided.

Additive CNS depression. Combining a benzodiazepine with a non-benzodiazepine Z-drug (both GABA-A modulators) increases risk of excessive sedation and respiratory depression. Not recommended.
Additive CNS depression and sedation. Both agents depress the CNS; concurrent use increases risk of excessive sedation and respiratory depression.

Bupropion lowers the seizure threshold; benzodiazepines have anticonvulsant properties that may partially offset this. Monitor for CNS effects.
Additive CNS depression possible. Buspirone does not enhance benzodiazepine effects at GABA-A receptors, but additive sedative effects may occur.
Additive CNS depression possible at higher doses. Generally minimal interaction at standard doses.
Additive sedation. Combining a benzodiazepine with a melatonin receptor agonist for sleep is generally redundant and may cause excessive sedation.
Additive CNS depression and sedation.

Minimal pharmacokinetic interaction. Temazepam undergoes primarily glucuronide conjugation (Phase II) with minimal CYP450 metabolism, so cimetidine's CYP inhibition has limited clinical impact.
Caffeine's CNS stimulant effects may partially antagonize the sedative and hypnotic effects of temazepam.
Ephedrine's sympathomimetic and stimulant effects may partially counteract the CNS depressant effects of temazepam.

medications.administration: Take 15-30 minutes before bedtime. May be taken with or without food. Use the lowest effective dose. Do not use for more than 7-10 days without reassessment. Do not abruptly discontinue; taper dose gradually to avoid withdrawal. Schedule IV controlled substance.

medications.mechanism_of_action: Irreversible cyclooxygenase-1 (COX-1) inhibitor and modifier of COX-2 enzymatic activity. Unlike other NSAIDs which bind reversibly, aspirin's acetyl group covalently and irreversibly modifies the COX enzymes. Irreversibly blocks thromboxane A2 synthesis on platelets, preventing platelet aggregation for the lifespan of the platelet (7-10 days). Provides anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and antipyretic effects via prostaglandin synthesis inhibition.
medications.half_life: 15-20 minutes (aspirin, rapidly hydrolyzed to salicylic acid); salicylic acid half-life is dose-dependent: 2-3 hours (low dose), up to 15-30 hours (high anti-inflammatory doses)
medications.duration_of_action: Analgesic/antipyretic: 4-6 hours; Antiplatelet: 7-10 days (irreversible COX-1 inhibition persists for the lifespan of the platelet)
medications.active_metabolites: Salicylic acid — the primary active metabolite responsible for anti-inflammatory and analgesic effects. Formed by rapid hydrolysis of aspirin by esterases in the GI mucosa, plasma, and liver.
medications.half_life_of_active_metabolites: 2-3 hours (salicylic acid, low dose); dose-dependent kinetics — up to 15-30 hours at high anti-inflammatory doses due to saturable metabolism

interactions_with_other_drugs_on_this_li.interaction: Ibuprofen may interfere with aspirin's irreversible antiplatelet effect on COX-1 if taken before aspirin (competitive antagonism at the COX-1 binding site). Additive risk of GI bleeding and renal toxicity. If both are needed, take aspirin at least 30 minutes before ibuprofen.

Concurrent NSAID use increases GI bleeding, renal, and cardiovascular risk. Naproxen has less interference with aspirin's antiplatelet effect compared to ibuprofen, but additive GI toxicity remains a concern.
Concurrent use increases GI and cardiovascular risk. Celecoxib's COX-2 selectivity does not eliminate additive GI bleeding risk when combined with aspirin.
Avoid concurrent use of multiple NSAIDs. Additive GI, renal, and cardiovascular toxicity with no added efficacy.
SSRIs impair platelet serotonin uptake; combined with aspirin's irreversible antiplatelet effect, GI bleeding risk is significantly increased (3-6 fold). Consider gastroprotection with a PPI.
Trazodone has serotonergic activity that may impair platelet function. Combined with aspirin, there is a modestly increased risk of bleeding.
NSAIDs including aspirin may reduce the antihypertensive effect of beta-blockers by inhibiting renal prostaglandin synthesis.
PPI provides gastroprotection during aspirin use. Beneficial combination to reduce risk of aspirin-induced GI bleeding, especially in high-risk patients.
Pantoprazole provides gastroprotection during aspirin therapy. Often co-prescribed to reduce risk of aspirin-induced gastropathy and peptic ulcers.
H2 blocker provides partial gastroprotection during aspirin use. Less effective than PPIs for preventing NSAID-induced ulcers but may reduce GI symptoms.
Colestipol may reduce aspirin absorption by binding it in the GI tract. Separate dosing by at least 1 hour before or 4 hours after colestipol.
Can be used together for additive analgesia via different mechanisms (COX inhibition vs. central analgesic effect). Generally safe combination. Common in OTC products.
Caffeine enhances aspirin's analgesic effect (adjuvant analgesic). This is a well-established beneficial combination commonly found in OTC pain products (e.g., Excedrin).
Cimetidine may increase salicylate levels by reducing renal clearance and inhibiting hepatic metabolism. Monitor for signs of salicylate toxicity with concurrent use.

medications.administration: Take with food, milk, or a full glass of water to reduce GI upset. Enteric-coated or buffered formulations may reduce stomach irritation. Do not crush or chew enteric-coated tablets. Chewable, non-enteric coated tablets are preferred when rapid onset is required (e.g., acute coronary syndrome). For cardiovascular prophylaxis, take at the same time each day.

medications.maximum_daily_dosage: 0.5 mg/night; 0.125-0.25 mg recommended for elderly or debilitated patients
medications.time_required_between_doses: Once nightly at bedtime; do not redose during the same night
medications.mechanism_of_action: Benzodiazepine hypnotic. Classified as a positive allosteric modulator of GABA-A receptors (GABA-PAM), with particular affinity for BNZ1 receptors which mediate sleep. Binds to benzodiazepine receptors on GABA-A chloride channel complexes in the CNS (limbic, thalamic, and hypothalamic regions), enhancing GABAergic inhibition by increasing chloride ion influx frequency. This leads to CNS depression, producing sedation, hypnosis, and decreased sleep latency. Metabolized primarily via CYP3A4-mediated hydroxylation to form 1-hydroxytriazolam and 4-hydroxytriazolam, which are subsequently conjugated to inactive glucuronides.
medications.time_to_max_concentration: Within 2 hours (oral); onset of action within 15-30 minutes
medications.duration_of_action: 6-7 hours (hypnotic effect; relatively short-acting compared to other benzodiazepines)
medications.active_metabolites: None — 1-hydroxytriazolam and 4-hydroxytriazolam are formed but are conjugated to pharmacologically inactive glucuronides and excreted primarily in urine

Esomeprazole inhibits CYP2C19 and weakly inhibits CYP3A4. Case reports describe interactions between PPIs and CYP3A4-metabolized benzodiazepines. Clinical significance is generally low but monitor for increased triazolam effects.
Unlike cimetidine, famotidine does not significantly inhibit CYP enzymes. Minimal pharmacokinetic interaction expected with triazolam.
